summary: Stancil argues that current AI/automation tools in analytics mostly disappoint because they’re built to mimic analysts’ existing tasks, which often require human judgment and context. He suggests a more promising future is AI as a creative partner—posing novel questions, reframing problems, or generating alternative structures—changing what analytics work even looks like.
money_quotes: Augmented analytics tools, which market themselves as needle detectors for our giant haystacks of data, find mostly useless correlations, some spurious ones, and the occasional meaningful one.
This is why analysts (should) get paid. They have to account for unaccountable factors.
In dismissing the potential of AI in analytics, I’m falling into the same trap that I did with Alexa.

The broader point is that this approach—use AI as a creative partner rather than a calculator for trudging through a bunch of repetitive math problems—is almost entirely different from how the industry has traditionally thought of applying it to analytics.
But, for better or for worse, I now believe it’s inevitable that we’ll get something.
key_insight: AI is unlikely to replace analysts by doing their current work, but it may transform analytics by acting as a creative partner that changes the questions and problems themselves.
